title: Slow Trains Home
passage:
For most of the 1990s, the sleeper train looked like a relic. One national operator after another quietly ___(1)___ its overnight services, reasoning that a traveller in a hurry would always take the early flight and be at the meeting by nine. The berths were cramped and ageing, the fares awkward to book across borders, and the longer routes ran at a loss that no transport ministry seemed willing to ___(2)___ indefinitely.

Then, almost without warning, the night train came back into fashion. When the operator Halden Rail relaunched its Aurora service between the capital and the northern coast in the winter of 2019, its planners expected to fill perhaps half the cabins on a good night. ___(3)___, the first season sold out weeks in advance, and a second set of carriages had to be leased from an operator two countries away. Part of the pull was a growing unease about short-haul flying; part was the plain convenience of boarding after dinner and waking beside the sea, a single bed crossing the mountains overnight sparing the traveller a wasted day at either end.

Yet the revival is a good deal more ___(4)___ than the cheering headlines suggest. Ingrid Sollander, a transport economist who has audited three of the new lines, points out that almost all of them survive on public subsidy, and that the carriages now being celebrated are decades old and costly to keep running. "Take away the grants and the nostalgia," she cautions, "and the ledgers look far less ___(5)___." The romance of the sleeper, she suggests, has for the moment outrun its arithmetic.
— Elias Thorne, transport columnist

questions.rationale: Collocation gap; all four are past-tense transitive verbs (POS-uniform). The frame is 'One national operator after another quietly ___ its overnight services' = took them out of operation: 'withdrew its services' is the locking collocation. dismissed = collocation_misfit (you dismiss a claim, a fear, or an employee, not your own train service). declined = sense/collocation_misfit (transitive 'decline' means refuse an offer; an operator does not 'decline its own services', and the reduce-sense of decline is intransitive so it cannot take 'its services' as an object). renounced = collocation_misfit (one renounces a claim, a title, or a faith, not a scheduled service). Only 'withdrew' is idiomatic; the other three are real verbs that fail on collocation or sense.

Collocation gap; all four are base-form verbs after 'willing to' (POS-uniform). The object is 'a loss ... no transport ministry seemed willing to ___ indefinitely' = keep funding or guaranteeing: 'underwrite a loss' is the idiom. condone = sense_misfit (condone requires a moral wrong to tolerate; a financial loss is not wrongdoing). overturn = collocation_misfit (one overturns a decision, a verdict, or a ruling, not a loss). dispel = collocation_misfit (dispel takes doubts, fears, or myths, not a loss). Only 'underwrite' collocates with a sustained financial loss.

Connective-logic gap; all four are sentence adverbs (connective class matched). The planners 'expected to fill perhaps half the cabins', and the gapped clause reports the opposite outcome ('sold out weeks in advance'), so the link is counter-to-expectation: 'Instead' is the only fit. Accordingly = wrong_logic (signals a consequence in line with the expectation, but the result defies it). Otherwise = wrong_logic (conditional 'if that were not so', absent here). Meanwhile = wrong_logic (temporal/parallel, and there is no second simultaneous event). Only 'Instead' carries the reversal.

Polarity gap with a suffix-rhymed -ious set. The contrastive pivot 'Yet ... more ___ than the cheering headlines suggest', reinforced downstream (survives on subsidy, ageing stock, costly upkeep), demands the negative pole = fragile/unstable: 'precarious'. auspicious = polarity_mirror (the positive pole a skimmer grabs, but 'Yet' plus the evidence of fragility forbids a favourable word). gregarious = sense_misfit (sociable, of a person or animal). meticulous = sense_misfit (careful, painstaking). Only 'precarious' fits both the polarity and the sense.

Collocation/sense gap; all four are adjectives (POS-uniform). Sollander says that stripped of grants and nostalgia 'the ledgers look far less ___', i.e. far less financially healthy: 'the ledgers look less rosy' is the idiom. hollow = collocation_misfit (a hollow victory or a hollow promise, not hollow ledgers). candid = sense_misfit (candid means frank; ledgers are not frank). generous = sense_misfit (a person or a portion is generous, not a set of accounts). Only 'rosy' collocates with a favourable financial picture, and 'far less rosy' is exactly the guarded reading her caution requires.

generator_meta.gap_marker_convention: ___(n)___ inline in passage; questions[] carries gap n as q_index n with prompt 'Gap (n)'
generator_meta.schema_note: 5 gaps => 5 questions[]; matches the cloze invariant, M-SCHEMA's 1-5 range, and the bands.json cloze prompt/option classes. Keys spread C/A/D/B/C. See NOTES.md.
